agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to quotes in a system where 'confirmed' already has an established meaning for orders. The existing definition ties 'confirmed' to payment completion and immutability. The new request uses 'confirmed' to mean verbal agreement without payment — a fundamentally different state.","bounded_context":"Two bounded contexts are involved: Order Fulfillment (where 'confirmed' is established) and Sales/Quoting (where 'confirmed' is being proposed). The term 'confirmed' would carry contradictory semantics across these contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. This is mutable — it can still be revised, expire, or be withdrawn before converting to an order."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A sales proposal with pricing and terms presented to a customer, awaiting acceptance."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A commitment to fulfill goods/services, created after payment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A 'confirmed' order is immutable — no modifications permitted after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment is a data integrity constraint. Violating it corrupts fulfillment state, financial records, and downstream logistics.","testable_assertion":"Given a confirmed order, any attempt to modify its line items, pricing, or delivery details MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A 'confirmed' order implies payment has been received.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The payment-confirmation coupling is a financial integrity constraint. An order entering fulfillment without payment creates unrecoverable financial exposure.","testable_assertion":"An order MUST NOT transition to 'confirmed' status unless a successful payment event has been recorded."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A 'confirmed quote' (as proposed) does NOT imply payment and is NOT immutable.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a sales workflow policy — the level of mutability allowed on a verbally-accepted quote is a business decision, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'confirmed' status can still be revised, expired, or withdrawn before order creation."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Developer queries all 'confirmed' entities across contexts and conflates paid-immutable orders with unpaid-mutable quotes.","expected_behavior":"System must never allow cross-context status queries to treat quote confirmation and order confirmation as equivalent states."},{"scenario":"A reporting dashboard shows 'confirmed' items and stakeholders assume all confirmed items are paid.","expected_behavior":"Finance team misreads revenue pipeline — verbal agreements counted as collected revenue."},{"scenario":"A sales agent sees 'confirmed' on a quote and assumes it behaves like a confirmed order (immutable, paid).","expected_behavior":"Agent stops following up on payment, believing it is already collected. Revenue leaks."},{"scenario":"An integration or event handler listens for 'confirmed' status transitions and cannot distinguish between quote confirmation and order confirmation.","expected_behavior":"Downstream systems (warehouse, shipping, invoicing) may incorrectly trigger fulfillment for unpaid quotes."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK. Overloading 'confirmed' creates ambiguity between 'verbally agreed' and 'paid.' This directly threatens revenue recognition accuracy. If any system, report, or person treats a confirmed quote as a confirmed order, unpaid items enter fulfillment — causing shipping costs with no offsetting revenue.","operational":"Significant confusion across teams. Fulfillment, finance, and sales would each interpret 'confirmed' differently. Event-driven architectures are especially vulnerable — a generic 'entity.confirmed' event becomes semantically ambiguous.","user":"Sales agents, finance teams, and warehouse staff all interact with 'confirmed' status. Using the same word for opposite financial states (paid vs. unpaid) guarantees misinterpretation. Training and documentation cannot reliably prevent this — the language itself is the hazard.","risk":"Domain language collision is a structural defect, not a cosmetic issue. It will produce bugs that pass code review because they look correct. It will produce reports that look accurate but are wrong. The damage compounds over time as more systems consume the ambiguous term.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Cannot trust 'confirmed' in reports — must always verify which entity type is confirmed. Revenue recognition errors are likely."},{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Confusion between quote confirmation (their action) and order confirmation (system action post-payment). May stop pursuing payment prematurely."},{"group":"Warehouse / Fulfillment","impact":"Risk of receiving fulfillment signals for unpaid quotes if event handlers or queries conflate the two meanings."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every developer touching status logic must remember that 'confirmed' means opposite things in different contexts. This is a permanent source of bugs."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"May receive shipments for unpaid items, or conversely, experience delays if systems reject legitimate confirmed orders due to confusion."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable for orders, and unpaid+mutable for quotes. These are mutually exclusive semantics assigned to the same term within the same system. This is not an ambiguity that can be resolved by interpretation — it is a structural collision that must be resolved before acceptance criteria can be defined.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed order' is clear and well-established","The proposed 'confirmed quote' semantics directly contradict the established meaning","This will cause cross-context confusion in events, queries, and reporting"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the bounded contexts are sufficiently separated in the codebase to prevent leakage","Whether event infrastructure distinguishes entity types when emitting status changes","The intended downstream behavior when a quote is 'confirmed'"],"assumptions":["The system shares a common status enum or status field pattern across entities","Events or queries may reference 'confirmed' status without qualifying the entity type"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already defined in the domain as: paid, immutable, queued for fulfillment. The feature request redefines 'confirmed' for quotes as: unpaid, mutable, verbally agreed. These are opposite states sharing the same name. This is a domain language collision that will produce financial, operational, and technical defects. The quote status should use a distinct term — e.g., 'accepted,' 'agreed,' 'verbally_committed,' or 'customer_approved' — that accurately describes the state without colliding with the established meaning of 'confirmed.'","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Sales","Fulfillment","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["A: Use 'confirmed' for both contexts, relying on entity type to disambiguate (HIGH RISK)","B: Rename the quote status to a distinct term like 'accepted' or 'customer_approved' that reflects verbal agreement without implying payment (RECOMMENDED)","C: Rename the order status away from 'confirmed' to something like 'paid_and_locked' (DISRUPTIVE — changes established language)"],"recommendation":"Option B. The quote status must use a different term. 'Accepted' or 'customer_approved' accurately describes verbal agreement, preserves the established meaning of 'confirmed' for orders, and eliminates the collision. This is not a naming preference — it is a domain correctness requirement."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reuse of 'confirmed' with contradictory semantics across entity types","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-2 — 'confirmed' is established as paid+immutable. Applying it to an unpaid, mutable quote violates the ubiquitous language and will cause systems and people to misinterpret state."}]}
